explanation_md: The `operator<<` overload matches `Season` and runs the `case autumn:` branch, writing `"autumn"` to `out`. The function returns `out`, so the chain `<< s << '\n'` works correctly and the final newline is also printed.

options.a: Line 6: the return type should be `std::ostream&`
options.b: Line 6: the parameter `p` should be `Pet&` not `Pet`
options.c: Line 8: `std::string token` should be declared outside the function
options.d: Line 15: `return in` should be `return in >> p`

explanation_md: Because `p` is passed by value, every assignment (`p = cat`, etc.) modifies only the local copy. The caller's `myPet` is never updated. Changing the parameter to `Pet& p` makes the function write back to the original variable.

options.a: Because `std::ostream` has no copy constructor, so it must be passed and returned by reference
options.b: So that multiple `<<` calls can be chained in one expression, e.g. `std::cout << a << b`
options.c: Because `void` return type is reserved for `operator>>` only
options.d: To allow the compiler to inline the function at the call site

explanation_md: Chaining works because `std::cout << a << b` is parsed as `(std::cout << a) << b`. The first `<<` must return the stream so the second `<<` has something to operate on. If the return type were `void`, the chain would fail to compile. Option (a) is also true but is the reason the parameter and return use reference, not why a reference is returned at all.

Each `<<` with a `Rank` calls the overload. `ace` matches the first `if`, printing `"ace"`. `three` matches the third `if`, printing `"three"`. A space literal is printed between them, and `'\n'` ends the line.
